shrine_notes: Also designated Ichinomiya of Musashi Province (武蔵国一宮); Sohonsha of approximately 280 Hikawa shrines across Saitama, Tokyo, and Kanagawa; listed as Myōjin Taisha (名神大社) in the Engishiki (927 CE); formerly ranked Kanpei Taisha (官幣大社) under the Meiji social ranking system. One of only 16 Chokusaisha nationwide. The city name Ōmiya (大宮) — meaning 'Great Shrine' — derives directly from this shrine.

deities.name: Susanoo no Mikoto
deities.domain: God of Storms, Seas & Purification; Slayer of Yamata no Orochi and pioneer of the land

deities.name: Inadahime no Mikoto
deities.domain: Goddess of Rice Fields & Feminine Grace; Divine consort of Susanoo

deities.name: Ashinazuchi no Mikoto
deities.domain: God of earthly protection; Father of Inadahime and divine ancestor of the Susa priestly lineage

deities.name: Tenazuchi no Mikoto
deities.domain: Goddess of earthly nurturing; Mother of Inadahime

shrine_notes: The only shrine in all of Japan enshrining Susanoo no Mikoto's actual divine soul (御魂, mitama) rather than a divided spirit (分霊, wakemitama). Listed in the Engishiki Jinmyōchō (延喜式神名帳, 927 CE). Formerly ranked Kokuhei Shōsha (国幣小社). Designated Izumo Koku Shinbutsu Reijō No. 18 (出雲國神仏霊場第十八番). The Reiwa Grand Shrine Renovation (令和の御遷宮) commenced April 2025.

kanji: 出雲大社（いづもおおやしろ）

deities.name: Ōkuninushi no Ōkami
deities.domain: God of Nation-Building, Medicine, Agriculture & All Bonds; Supreme sovereign of the invisible world and master of musubi — the divine force that binds all living things

shrine_notes: Ichinomiya of Izumo Province (出雲国一之宮). Head shrine of the Izumo Taisha-kyō (出雲大社教) religious organization — one of very few shrines operating simultaneously under its own religious body and the Jinja Honchō. Formerly ranked Kanpei Taisha (官幣大社) — the highest rank in the Meiji shrine system. The main hall (御本殿) is a National Treasure (国宝). Correct formal reading is 'Izumo Ōyashiro' (いづもおおやしろ), not 'Izumo Taisha.' Uses unique worship etiquette of 2 bows, 4 claps, 1 bow (二礼四拍手一礼) unlike the standard 2-2-1 used at most shrines.

deities.name: Ōmononushi no Ōkami
deities.domain: God of Nation-Building, Medicine, Agriculture, Brewing & Purification; Divine guardian of the Yamato land and sovereign of the sacred mountain Miwa

deities.name: Ōnamuji no Kami
deities.domain: God of Nation-Building & Earthly Prosperity; Co-builder of the land alongside Sukunabikona

deities.name: Sukunabikona no Kami
deities.domain: God of Medicine, Brewing & Agriculture; Divine partner of Ōnamuji in the building of the land

shrine_notes: Ichinomiya of Yamato Province (大和国一之宮). One of the Nijūnisha (二十二社), the 22 shrines specially designated by the Heian imperial court to receive offerings during national crises — ranked Chūshichisha (中七社, middle seven). Listed as Myōjin Taisha (名神大社) in the Engishiki (927 CE). Formerly ranked Kanpei Taisha (官幣大社) — the highest rank under the Meiji shrine system. Has no main hall (本殿) — Mt. Miwa (三輪山) itself is the divine body (御神体). The Mitsutorii (三ツ鳥居, triple torii) inside the precinct is a National Important Cultural Property. Origin shrine of all Miwa/Ōmononushi shrines across Japan.

kanji: 金刀比羅宮（金毘羅大権現）

deities.name: Ōmononushi no Kami
deities.domain: God of Nation-Building, Agriculture, Medicine, Brewing & Maritime Protection; sovereign of the sea and guardian of all who travel upon water

deities.name: Emperor Sutoku
deities.domain: Deified sovereign; pacified spirit of the exiled emperor who died in Sanuki; guardian of those who suffer unjust misfortune
deities.title: Emperor Sutoku (崇徳天皇)

shrine_notes: Sohonsha of approximately 600 Kotohira shrines (金刀比羅神社・琴平神社) across Japan. Head shrine of Kotohira Honkyō (金刀比羅本教), an independent religious organization. Withdrew from the Jinja Honchō in 2020 and currently operates as an independent shrine (単立神社) — the only shrine of this status in our database. Chokusaisha designation was granted by imperial decree on September 13, 1868 and has not been formally revoked. Formerly ranked Kokuhei Chūsha (国幣中社) under the Meiji shrine system. The Omote Shoin (表書院) and its four-legged gate are designated National Important Cultural Properties, containing fusuma paintings by Maruyama Ōkyo and Itō Jakuchū.
